message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>Comparing SARS-CoV-2 infection-induced gene expression signatures to drug treatment-induced gene expression signatures is a promising bioinformatic tool to repurpose existing drugs against SARS-CoV-2. The general hypothesis of signature based drug repurposing is that drugs with inverse similarity to a disease signature can reverse disease phenotype and thus be effective against it. However, in the case of viral infection diseases, like SARS-CoV-2, infected cells also activate adaptive, antiviral pathways, so that the relationship between effective drug and disease signature can be more ambiguous.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  To address this question, we analysed gene expression data from
                  <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                  SARS-CoV-2 infected cell lines, and gene expression signatures of drugs showing anti-SARS-CoV-2 activity. Our extensive functional genomic analysis showed that both infection and treatment with
                  <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                  effective drugs leads to activation of antiviral pathways like NFkB and JAK-STAT. Based on the similarity - and not inverse similarity - between drug and infection-induced gene expression signatures, we were able to predict the
                  <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                  antiviral activity of drugs. We also identified SREBF1/2, key regulators of lipid metabolising enzymes, as the most activated transcription factors by several
                  <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                  effective antiviral drugs. Using a fluorescently labeled cholesterol sensor, we showed that these drugs decrease the cholesterol levels of plasma-membrane. Supplementing drug-treated cells with cholesterol reversed the
                  <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                  antiviral effect, suggesting the depleting plasma-membrane cholesterol plays a key role in virus inhibitory mechanism.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>Our results can help to more effectively repurpose approved drugs against SARS-CoV-2, and also highlights key mechanisms behind their antiviral effect.</jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Abstract Figure</jats:title>
                  <jats:fig id="ufig1" position="float" fig-type="figure" orientation="portrait">
                    <jats:graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="459786v1_ufig1" position="float" orientation="portrait"/>
                  </jats:fig>
                </jats:sec>
title: Computational drug repurposing against SARS-CoV-2 reveals plasma membrane cholesterol depletion as key factor of antiviral drug activity
